articles.author: Lorian Belanger, Brian P. D. Hannon
articles.description: Explosions sounded over Dubai early Tuesday as the United Arab Emirates’ military worked to intercept incoming Iranian fire that caused the country to briefly close its airspace as Israel launched new strikes in war in the Middle East.
articles.title: Live updates: UAE briefly closes airspace over Iranian attacks as Israel strikes Beirut and Tehran

articles.description: Iranian authorities have confirmed the death of Ali Larijani, a top Iranian security official who was thought to be running the country since the beginning of the war. Larijani was a top security official and a hard-liner who had been appointed to advise Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on strategy in nuclear talks with the Trump administration. He traveled to Oman to meet with mediators just two weeks before the U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed Khamenei and started the war late last month. Larijani was born into one of Iran’s most famous political families but was ineligible to become supreme leader because he wasn't a Shiite cleric.
articles.title: Ali Larijani, one of Iran's top security officials, is killed in an Israeli strike

articles.author: Didi Tang
articles.description: Analysts say China is unlikely to help the U.S. reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and it is probably welcoming a delay in President Donald Trump’s Beijing trip. The Iran war has dragged into a third week and oil shipments through the strait are stalled. U.S. allies are also resisting deeper involvement. Experts have expressed concerns that China stands to gain if the U.S. gets bogged down in the war. Chinese diplomats are courting Middle East partners and have sent humanitarian aid to families of children and teachers killed in the bombing of an elementary school in Iran.
articles.title: China unlikely to help US reopen Hormuz as Trump’s Beijing trip slips

articles.author: Aamer Madhani, Darlene Superville, Michelle L. Price
articles.description: President Donald Trump says NATO and other allies have rejected his calls to help secure the Strait of Hormuz. He complained on Tuesday that he has been unable to rally support behind the war of choice that he insists he’s conducting for the good of the world even if it doesn’t appreciate his effort. Trump’s indignant response to allies’ rejection to get involved in the war further underscored that the Iran war, now in its third week and causing reverberations across the global economy, is one that the international community is looking for the U.S. leader to sort out himself. And it's a dynamic the president insists he’s OK with.
articles.title: Trump fumes at NATO for refusing to help secure Strait of Hormuz

articles.author: Jon Gambrell
articles.description: Satellite photos are beginning to be released showing ships ablaze in an Iranian port and destroyed buildings at military bases. The images come from Planet Labs PBC, a San Francisco-based firm used widely by media outlets, including The Associated Press. Planet Labs has said it will put a two-week delay on its imagery becoming public citing the “increasingly complex regional security environment” and concerns its imagery could be used by “adversarial actors.” Information about the damage done across the wider Middle East in the Iran war remains scarce.
articles.title: Satellite photos show damage in Iran war

articles.author: Bill Barrow
articles.description: The Trump counterterrorism official who resigned Tuesday had been a staunch supporter of the president through his 2020 election defeat, the Jan. 6 riots and years of conservative media advocacy and failed congressional bids. But the president's war in Iran and his alliance with Israel against the Islamic clerics who led the Tehran government were too much for Joe Kent. Kent's resignation deprives Trump of a once-staunch ally and also highlights the former Army Green Beret's previous ties to antisemitism and right-wing extremism. Kent had 11 combat tours, mostly in Iraq, but became an opponent of U.S. interventionism after his wife was killed in Syria in 2019.
articles.title: What to know about Joe Kent, Trump's former counterterrorism chief

articles.author: Sophia Tareen
articles.description: Illinois voters are deciding primaries for six open U.S. House and Senate seats that will spur a new generation of leadership in the state’s heavily Democratic congressional delegation. The retirement of Democratic Sen. Dick Durbin has triggered a competitive campaign to find a replacement. Among the candidates in Tuesday's Democratic primary are two sitting House members and the lieutenant governor. The race is also a test of the influence of Democratic Gov. JB Pritzker. House retirements created open seats across the Chicago area. The American Israel Public Affairs Committee and PACs supporting the cryptocurrency and AI industries also have spent big on several of the contests. The stakes are high, with most primary winners in the Democratic stronghold expected to win in November.
articles.title: Illinois voters pick new slate of Democratic leaders in Tuesday primary

articles.author: Ali Swenson
articles.description: The Trump administration is expanding its growing Medicaid fraud crackdown to Florida. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services Administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz on Tuesday asked Florida state officials to explain how they find and stop bad actors in the health care program. Oz said Florida has long been a hotspot for health care fraud and its leaders need to ‘step up’ to help stop it. Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier said he looks forward to working with Oz on the issue. The request follows a Monday executive order from President Donald Trump to create an anti-fraud task force across federal benefit programs. Florida is at least the fifth state this year that has received such a letter from Oz. It's the first Republican-led state to receive a publicized inquiry.
articles.title: Trump administration targets Medicaid fraud in Florida

articles.description: A judge threw a government attorney out of a hearing and ordered the three officials in charge of New Jersey’s U.S. Attorney’s Office to answer his questions under oath. U.S. District Judge Zahid N. Quraishi was vexed by the Justice Department’s chaotic oversight of federal prosecutions in the state. Quraishi lit into prosecutors after another judge ruled last week that the Trump administration’s decision to replace interim U.S. Attorney Alina Habba with a trio of officials was another violation of the Constitution’s Appointments Clause, which requires Senate confirmation. The officials, Philip Lamparello, Jordan Fox and Ari Fontecchio, have remained in charge pending an appeal.
articles.title: Judge throws prosecutor out of court and orders leaders of NJ’s US Attorney’s Office to testify

articles.author: Alanna Durkin Richer
articles.description: Attorney General Pam Bondi has been subpoenaed to answer questions from Congress about the Justice Department’s sex trafficking investigation of Jeffrey Epstein and the agency’s handling of millions of files related to the disgraced financier. Bondi was ordered Tuesday to appear for a deposition on April 14 by the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform after a vote earlier this month that was supported by five Republicans. The Justice Department’s failure to fend off the subpoena from the Republican-led committee underscores widespread discontent among President Donald Trump’s own base over Bondi’s management of the review and release of a trove of documents from the criminal investigation into Epstein.
articles.title: Pam Bondi subpoenaed by Congress over Epstein files
